error.signature: AI tells a foreign visitor to Japan to walk into a bank and open an account like in their home country
error.regex: (japan.*bank.*account|japanese.*bank.*open|open.*account.*japan|일본.*계좌|日本.*銀行.*口座|zairyu.*card|在留カード.*口座)

verdict.summary: Most major Japanese banks (MUFG, SMBC, Mizuho) require foreign residents to hold a residence card (在留カード) with at least 6 months remaining on the visa before opening a standard account. Many branches additionally require a my-number (マイナンバー) notification card and proof of a Japanese address. Japan Post Bank (ゆうちょ) and Shinsei are the usual exceptions for newcomers.

dead_ends.action: Walk into any Japanese bank on day 1 with just a passport and try to open a regular account
dead_ends.why_fails: Under Financial Services Agency anti-money-laundering guidance, most high-street banks refuse applicants who have been in Japan fewer than 6 months. A tourist visa passport alone is rejected at the counter — staff will ask for a residence card.
dead_ends.condition: New arrival with no residence card yet

dead_ends.action: Assume an English-speaking mega-bank branch will accept the application without a Japanese-address proof
dead_ends.why_fails: Even English-support branches require a juminhyo (住民票) or utility bill confirming a Japanese address. Hotel addresses are rejected. The my-number notification slip (通知カード) or card is required at most branches after 2016.

workarounds.action: Start with Japan Post Bank (ゆうちょ銀行) using only passport + residence card; most branches accept applicants even under the 6-month threshold.
workarounds.condition: Have a residence card and a Japanese address

workarounds.action: If staying less than 6 months, consider a Shinsei Bank (SBI Shinsei) account — they advertise shorter-stay eligibility and have English online banking.
workarounds.condition: Short-term residency, need online banking in English
workarounds.tradeoff: Some branches still require 6 months; call ahead.

workarounds.action: Always prepare: residence card, my-number card or notification slip, proof of Japanese address (juminhyo or utility bill), and a hanko (seal) for traditional branches.
